student_name: Eliane Portela Nogueira Lima de Brito
hub_subtitle: Business English for a doctor rebuilding her professional voice

lesson.menu_title: The Story of the Comeback — Telling How You Got Here
lesson.menu_desc: Narrative tenses and the past perfect continuous (By the time it took off, we had been working for years) to tell the story of a career the way a founder does -- 31 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 16 -- The Story of the Comeback
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Eliane Portela | Aula 16 -- The Story of the Comeback: Telling How You Got Here

vocab.title: Match each word to its meaning
defs: a moment when an important change happens
the basic preparation done before the real work
the force that keeps a process growing once it starts
a sudden, important success or discovery
to suddenly start to succeed or grow fast
to bring a good result after effort
from the very beginning, from nothing
to become fully effective or successful
courage and determination over a long time
developing over time, not yet finished but on the way

paras: By the time Eliane finally opened her clinic, she had been working toward it for the better part of a decade. Most people saw only the launch; they did not see the years of groundwork that had come before. She had been building the plan quietly, one partnership at a time, long before the doors ever opened. The early days were an uphill battle. While the bigger clinics were scaling fast, she was still assembling a team from the ground up, and more than once she wondered whether the whole thing would ever take off.

The turning point came in the second year. A procedure that her team had been refining for months finally produced a breakthrough, and suddenly the momentum she had been chasing arrived almost on its own. Against the odds, the clinic that everyone had written off began to come into its own. Looking back, Eliane could see that nothing had happened overnight: the success that seemed sudden had actually been in the making for years. What the market called luck was really grit -- the quiet, stubborn work she had been doing while no one was watching.

There is a lesson in the way she tells the story now. When she describes the comeback, she does not start at the moment it paid off. She starts earlier, with the season when she had been struggling and the plan had not yet worked. That is what makes a good story of a career: the listener needs to know what had been happening before the turning point, so that the breakthrough means something. By the time the momentum finally took off, Eliane had learned that the years of groundwork were not the boring part of the story -- they were the story.
reading.source: Adapted from a founder's essay on telling your own story, The Long Road

reading.prompt: What is the main idea of the text?
choices: The clinic was an overnight success, and the years before the launch did not really matter to the story Eliane tells.

A good story of a career starts before the turning point: the listener needs to know what had been happening during the years of groundwork and struggle, so that the breakthrough finally means something.
The text argues that luck, not work, is what made the clinic come into its own in the second year.

items: Eliane had been working toward the clinic for only a few months before it opened.
The text says that by the time she opened, she had been working toward it for the better part of a decade.
The turning point came when a procedure her team had been refining finally produced a breakthrough.
The text says the turning point came in the second year, when a procedure the team had been refining for months finally produced a breakthrough.
Eliane believes the clinic's success happened overnight.
The text says nothing had happened overnight: the success that seemed sudden had actually been in the making for years.
When Eliane tells the story now, she starts at the moment it paid off.
The text says she does not start at the moment it paid off; she starts earlier, with the season when she had been struggling.

parts: When I tell our story, I do not begin at the launch. I begin years earlier, when we
we had been chasing finally arrived.

gapfill.text: Notice how the words carry a journey. You lay the groundwork, you fight an uphill battle, you build from the ground up, and against the odds you keep going with grit. Then the turning point arrives: a breakthrough gives you momentum, the clinic comes into its own, and the work that had been in the making for years finally pays off and takes off. This is the language a founder uses to tell the story of how she got here.

bank.label: Your storytelling toolkit
items: By the time we launched, we had been working on the plan for two years.
While the bigger clinics were scaling fast, we were still building from the ground up.
The turning point came when a procedure we had been refining produced a breakthrough.
Against the odds, the clinic slowly began to come into its own.
The success looked sudden, but it had been in the making for a long time.
What people called luck was really grit and years of groundwork.
Before the momentum took off, we had been struggling for months.
We had been laying the groundwork long before anyone saw the results.

lf.title: Narrative tenses: name each one
items: By the time we opened, we

lf.text: Each line is a different narrative tense. A is the past perfect continuous (had been + -ing): an action going on for a while up to a point in the past -- it stresses how long. B is the past continuous (was / were + -ing): a longer background action in progress. C is the past simple (came): the single, completed events that move the story forward. D is the past perfect (had chased): an action finished before another past moment. E is also past perfect, but with a state verb (be), which does not take the continuous. Good storytelling mixes all four: the past simple carries the sequence, while the others fill in what had been happening around it.

items.situation: Tell the very beginning of your professional story. Use the past perfect continuous to say how long you had been working before your first real result.
tips: By the time I opened the clinic, I had been planning it for almost ten years.
I had been working nights on the project long before anyone noticed it.

items.situation: Describe your turning point. Use the past simple for the event and the past perfect for what had happened before it.
tips: The turning point came in the second year, after we had signed our first partner.
Everything changed when a procedure we had refined finally produced a breakthrough.

items.situation: Explain a breakthrough that only came after an uphill battle. Use 'against the odds' and 'from the ground up'.
tips: Against the odds, the clinic we had built from the ground up finally took off.
It was an uphill battle, but the breakthrough came and the momentum paid off.

items.situation: Say what had been happening in the background while you were building. Use the past continuous and the past perfect continuous.
tips: While the market was scaling fast, we had been laying the groundwork quietly.
The bigger clinics were growing, but we had been refining one procedure for months.

items: Tell the years before your first success. Use the past perfect continuous (I had been working / building / refining) to describe what had been happening long before the launch.
Describe the moment everything changed. Use the past simple for the event (the turning point came, the breakthrough happened) and the past perfect for what had already happened before it.
Tell how, against the odds, the thing you built from the ground up began to come into its own. Show the momentum finally taking off and paying off.
Round 4 -- The story you tell now
Put it together in one minute: start before the turning point, describe what had been in the making, and land on the breakthrough -- so the comeback means something.

answerkey.title: Reveal the model language
list: The groundwork years: 'For almost a decade before the clinic opened, I had been laying the groundwork -- building partnerships, refining procedures, hiring a team from the ground up.'
The turning point: 'The turning point came in the second year. We had been refining one procedure for months when it finally produced a breakthrough.'
The comeback: 'Against the odds, the clinic everyone had written off began to come into its own. The momentum I had been chasing finally took off, and the years of work paid off.'
The story you tell now: 'I never start at the launch. I start earlier, when nothing had worked yet, because the years that had been in the making are what make the breakthrough mean something.'
answerkey.note: Notice how each answer mixes the narrative tenses: the past simple (came, took off) carries the events, while the past perfect (had written off) and the past perfect continuous (had been refining / chasing / laying) fill in what had been happening around them. That is how a founder turns a career into a story.

listenings.text: Welcome back to the show. Today's guest is a founder whose story almost did not happen. For years, long before anyone had heard her name, she had been building something quietly, from the ground up. When I first met her, the clinic was still an uphill battle. She had been working nights, she had been losing sleep, and the momentum she needed had not yet arrived. Then came the turning point. A procedure her team had been refining for months finally produced a breakthrough, and against the odds, the whole thing began to take off. Here is what I love about her story. By the time the world called it an overnight success, she had already been at it for the better part of a decade. The groundwork was the story. Let's hear it in her own words.

When people ask how the clinic started, I never begin at the launch. I begin earlier, in the years when I had been laying the groundwork and nothing had worked yet. I had been building the plan one partnership at a time, and honestly, it was an uphill battle. While the bigger clinics were scaling fast, I was still hiring my first team from the ground up. The turning point came in the second year. We had been refining one procedure for months when it finally produced a breakthrough, and the momentum I had been chasing arrived almost overnight. Against the odds, the clinic came into its own. Looking back, the success that seemed sudden had really been in the making for years. What they called luck, I call grit.
